candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be operationalized into a predictive model of 'interaction structure' that distinguishes, ex ante, when a given technological wave will generate diffusion-dominant versus capture-dominant feedback loops? The open problem is identifying the minimal load-bearing variables, thresholds, and causal topology—across ownership, bargaining power, market concentration, institutional lag, and diffusion channels—rather than leaving the explanation at an evocative but under-specified systems level.

What determines whether key complementarities are inherently scarce versus made scarce by policy, governance, and market design? This is unresolved because the conjecture treats scarcity of control points (compute, data, platforms, IP, distribution) as central, but does not yet explain which bottlenecks are technologically necessary, which are contingent artifacts, and how they can be structurally transformed without destroying innovation incentives.

What are the dominant time-scale dynamics between technological acceleration and institutional adaptation, and are there tipping points beyond which concentrated gains become self-reinforcing and difficult to reverse? The conjecture relies on adaptation speed as a load-bearing factor, but leaves open how lag, hysteresis, and path dependence work in practice, including whether early concentration irreversibly shapes later diffusion opportunities.
